pages.text:
Overview
Terraform (HashiCorp) is a declarative IaC tool using HCL to define, plan, and apply
infrastructure across providers (AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, and hundreds more) with a
consistent workflow. State tracking makes it distinct from purely imperative scripting.
Core Workflow
Command
Purpose
terraform init
Downloads providers/modules, configures backend
terraform plan
Computes diff between desired config and current state — no
changes applied
terraform apply
Executes the plan, creates/updates/destroys real resources
terraform destroy
Tears down all resources tracked in state
terraform validate
Checks configuration syntax and internal consistency
terraform fmt
Auto-formats HCL to canonical style
State Management
Remote backend — store state in Azure Storage / S3 / Terraform Cloud, never commit
to git
State locking — prevents concurrent applies corrupting state (native in Azure Storage
+ S3/DynamoDB combo)
Workspaces — isolate state per environment (dev/staging/prod) within one
configuration
terraform import — brings existing, manually-created resources under Terraform
management
Drift detection — plan against real infra surfaces out-of-band manual changes
C L O U D C H E AT S H E E T · I A C
Terraform
Declarative infrastructure-as-code, state management, and multi-cloud provisioning
• 
• 
• 
• 
• 
Mazhar Khan — Cloud Security Portfolio
Page 1 of 3

Language Building Blocks
Block
Purpose
provider
Configures the target platform (auth, region/subscription)
resource
Declares a manageable infrastructure object
data
Reads existing infrastructure without managing its lifecycle
variable / output
Parameterize input and expose values for chaining modules
module
Reusable, composable bundle of resources
locals
Named expressions for DRY configuration
Security Best Practices
Never hardcode secrets — pull from Key Vault/Secrets Manager via data sources at
plan time
Least-privilege CI identity — the service principal/role running apply should be
scoped tightly, with plan-only access on PRs
State encryption — enable encryption at rest on the remote backend; state can
contain sensitive attributes
Policy as code — Sentinel (Terraform Cloud/Enterprise) or Open Policy Agent (OPA) /
Checkov for pre-apply guardrails
Module pinning — pin provider and module versions to avoid unreviewed drift on re-
init
Plan review gate — require human/automated review of the plan output before apply
in CI/CD
CI/CD Pattern
PR opened → pipeline runs fmt , validate , plan , policy scan (Checkov/tfsec) → plan output posted
as PR comment for review → on merge, pipeline runs apply using a scoped service principal with
remote state locking to prevent concurrent runs.
Common Pitfalls
Pitfall
Fix
Always use a remote backend with locking and encryption at rest

Page 2 of 3

Local state file
committed to git
Manual console
changes causing
drift
Enforce IaC-only changes via policy; run scheduled drift-detection
plans
No plan review
before apply in CI/
CD
Require plan output review/approval gate before merge-triggered
apply
Unpinned provider/
module versions
Pin versions with pessimistic constraints; review changelogs before
bumping
Monolithic single-
state configuration
Split by blast radius/lifecycle (network, platform, app) into separate
state files
Interview / Exam Quick Hits
terraform plan is read-only against the provider API; only apply mutates infrastructure
count and for_each both create multiple resource instances — for_each is preferred for
stability when items can be added/removed from the middle of a set
Provider version constraints use pessimistic operator ( ~> ) to allow patch updates
without breaking changes
terraform state mv refactors resource addresses without destroying/recreating
infrastructure
Remote state output values can be consumed cross-configuration via 
terraform_remote_state data source
